title: Should the baseline-approval workflow be implemented as a new side-flag on the u
question: Should the baseline-approval workflow be implemented as a new side-flag on the ui-review run artifact (reference-impl-only change) or as a new state-machine transition in the Standard schema, and what is the impact on existing Standard consumers?
findings: Three evidence sources converge on the same answer. (1) Standard state-machine structure. standard/state-machines/task.json defines the ui-review state with exactly two outbound transitions: to qa (agent, pass) and back to implementing (agent, bounce). Adding a new state — e.g. baselines-pending-approval — would require: (a) adding the state name to the status enum in standard/schemas/task.schema.json (additionalProperties: false; no other extension point exists at the top level), (b) adding the new transitions to standard/state-machines/task.json, (c) updating the Standard package validators (@cloverleaf/standard), and (d) updating every consumer that calls advanceWorkItemStatus or loadStateMachine, including reference-impl/lib/task.ts and reference-impl/lib/work-item.ts. This is a multi-file, breaking Standard change with broad consumer surface. (2) RFC CLV-9 scope constraints. The RFC's out_of_scope list explicitly reads: 'Modifying the Standard task state-machine to add new states for baseline approval.' Its acceptance_criteria explicitly reads: 'All changes are contained within reference-impl (prompt, config, lib); no modifications to the Standard schemas or state-machine JSON are required.' The RFC therefore pre-answers the question and the spike's role is to confirm the feasibility and mechanism of the reference-impl-only path. (3) Reference-impl artifact surface. The UI Reviewer writes its result to .cloverleaf/runs/{taskId}/ui-review/ (per ui-reviewer.md). The feedback envelope (feedback.schema.json) has additionalProperties: false at the top level but the finding.metadata object is unconstrained (additionalProperties: true). A baselines_pending flag can be surfaced as a top-level field in a separate sidecar file (e.g. .cloverleaf/runs/{taskId}/ui-review/state.json) that the reference-impl orchestration skills (cloverleaf-ui-review, cloverleaf-qa, cloverleaf-merge) read before advancing the task. Alternatively, since the ui-reviewer prompt already controls what it writes, the flag can be a key in that sidecar rather than inside the feedback envelope, keeping the Standard feedback schema untouched. The skill layer (reference-impl only) enforces the gate: if baselines_pending is true, the transition from ui-review to qa is blocked until a human runs an approve-baselines action that writes baselines_pending: false. No Standard schema needs to change.
recommendation: Implement the baseline-approval workflow as a reference-impl-only side-flag on the ui-review run artifact. Concretely: the UI Reviewer prompt writes a sidecar file .cloverleaf/runs/{taskId}/ui-review/state.json containing {"baselines_pending": true} whenever any new-baseline or dimension-mismatch result is produced; the cloverleaf-ui-review skill reads this file after the agent completes and, if baselines_pending is true, leaves the task in ui-review status and surfaces a human-facing message explaining that baselines require approval; a new approve-baselines action (human-triggered, reference-impl skill) writes baselines_pending: false to the sidecar and then allows the ui-review → qa transition to proceed. This approach: (a) requires zero changes to any Standard schema or state-machine JSON, satisfying RFC CLV-9's explicit out_of_scope constraint and its acceptance criterion; (b) has no impact on existing Standard consumers — they continue to see only the legal ui-review → qa or ui-review → implementing transitions; (c) is fully reversible — the sidecar file is a reference-impl implementation detail that can be removed or redesigned in a later version without a Standard version bump; (d) keeps the Standard's state graph clean and avoids proliferating states for implementation-level concerns. The cost is that the baselines_pending gate is invisible to any non-reference-impl consumer of the Standard, but this is acceptable because baseline approval is an artifact of the reference-impl's visual-diff feature, not a concern of the Standard's delivery model.
